This historical fragment is elaborated by
in a first-person narrative that firmly orients the reader. "The narrator is a person rather than male or female, but it was necessary that she not be a repetition of
Inventive methods of survival were taken from a combination of coastal lore and the author's imagination.

Lamps were made by the girl from the dried bodies of little fish called "sai-sai"[sic]. These silver fish come swimming out of the sea in schools so thick you can almost walk on them "on nights when the moon shines full", [sic] says the girl.
This is an example of the sense of reality and time placement the adventure story gives. The girl could not call the fish grunion, as we know them, or the story would be out the window, said
She measured time by suns and moons, and first marked the seasons, then only the years, and at last made no record at all.
The girl avoids the easy label of "earthquake" by saying "the earth rose and fell like a great animal breathing."
, intensely conscious of his craft, has an effortless style in the story designed for both adults and children. "The book wrote itself," he said, "and this is the only time it has ever happened."
has been book editor of the
last fall after five or six years there. He is an authority on California history, and wrote "Hill of the Hawk," a regional novel. An uninteresting area is likely to be dismissed by
Only Anglo Saxon words are used in the lyric prose of "Island of the Blue Dolphins." Good writing has been defined as talk written down and O'Dell's language sounds well read aloud.

There is a classic simplicity in this story of the sea. Few sea stories can afford to meander down unnecessary literary paths; something fundamental in the sea rejects unessentials. The object of speaking or writing should be to make something clear. If this does not happen, the story is pointless no matter how pretty the prose.
O'Dell's prize winning novel is as clear as the poster on the corner illuminated by innate good taste.

The ways of coastal living and knowledge of plants and animals set down in a book by
were not gained from one. Great mounds of abalone shells shining in the sun were seen by
when he was a boy watching the harvesting of the sea near
in the days before there were conservation laws. "You could chew on a dried abalone all day," he remembered.
The trays of dying meat destined for
covered 25 or 30 acres. The sights and sounds of these years led to Karana's fanciful scarecrow of abalone shells on
. The shells were hung on poles. Their insides were bright, they caught the sun, and turned one way and another in the wind.
It is doubtful that anyone could grow too old to be delighted by the ingenuity of a whale rib fence to rebuff wild dogs. "I just wondered how one could be protected on an island with no wood," said
"It was important to me to say the things in the book," said
, "we are in danger of losing the qualities of independence and self-reliance."
He speaks unhappily of an age of specialization when lawyers can speak only of torts, and admires his
neighbors who have practical, diversified knowledge. One neighbor has built a 5,000 square foot house by himself, including plastered ceilings and electrical wiring. Another invented a nasal spray.
who doesn't complain of a lack of literary neighbors.

, despite his admiration of self-reliance, would rather write than tend his 30-acre apple orchard or split wood although he does this occasionally. "Wood is $35 a cord, but can be split at $7 a cord," he remarked.

who had five or six suits of armor and discovered
came. He was named Alar Con and called himself the "son of the sun," thereby impressing early California residents considerably.
The book[,] which won the Newberry [sic] Award[,] also is entered in the Hans Christian Anderson [Andersen] international contest. "Island of the Blue Dolphins" will be around a long time.
The highly regarded Newberry [sic] award insures the life of a book; the story's topic had wide general interest for all ages; and it is beautifully written.
